steps.title: Identify Prospero's logical inference
steps.content: Prospero confirms Miranda's memory of the women is real (“Thou hadst, and more”), then immediately poses a logical chain: “If thou remember'st ought ere thou camest here, / How thou camest here thou mayst.” He is drawing a direct inference—if she can remember ANY experience from before she arrived, then she may be able to remember her arrival itself.

steps.title: Match the inference to the choices
steps.content:
Prospero's point is that Miranda's ability to recall an experience before arriving on the island suggests she may also recall her arrival itself.

A, B, and D either deny the memory's reality, introduce emotional tones unmentioned in the text, or mischaracterize doubt as reluctance—none capture the if-then inference Prospero draws.

This matches choice C — C is correct.
